title: Quantum computing in transition - Nature Biotechnology
gist: The first practical quantum advantage in biotech will come from hybrid quantum-classical systems, not fully quantum machines, with IBM projecting 2026 as the inflection point and fault-tolerant machines estimated as early as 2030; meanwhile 'harvest now, decrypt later' threats to genomic data and concentration of access among US, European and Chinese incumbents create security and equity fault lines.

themes.theme: Hybrid systems as the near-term path to quantum advantage

insights.claim: The first real-world quantum advantage in biotechnology will come from hybrid quantum-classical platforms rather than fully quantum machines, which serve as a bridge until full-scale fault-tolerant computers arrive.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Investors should watch hybrid-system vendors and pharma partnerships now rather than waiting for a full quantum machine; near-term commercial value accrues to companies integrating quantum processors with classical infrastructure.
insights.quote: quantum advantage — the state in which a quantum computer solves a practical, real-world problem faster or more efficiently than the most powerful classical supercomputer — is unlikely to emerge from a fully quantum machine at first
insights.locator: The year 2025 was declared the International Year

insights.claim: IBM predicted in January 2026 that 2026 will be the inflection point at which true quantum advantage is achieved using hybrid systems.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A dated, named forecast from IBM gives investors a concrete near-term catalyst window to test against actual 2026 milestones.
insights.quote: IBM predicted that 2026 will be the inflection point where we will see a true quantum advantage using hybrid systems
insights.locator: In fact, there have already been exciting developments

insights.claim: Hybrid quantum-classical systems tightly couple classical and quantum hardware in iterative loops where only small parts of the computation run on quantum hardware while the classical computer handles error correction, optimization and data processing.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Clarifies where value sits in the stack: classical orchestration and error-mitigation software are as investable as the qubit hardware itself.
insights.quote: Classical computers and quantum computers are tightly coupled in iterative loops where only small parts of the computation are performed on the quantum hardware
insights.locator: Hybrid systems use quantum error mitigation

insights.claim: Hybrid systems are not likely to scale to the qubit counts required for the use cases projected to benefit most, such as molecular simulation, drug design, and protein or RNA folding.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Hybrid approaches are a transitional bet with a ceiling; the largest end markets remain gated on fault-tolerant hardware, so hybrid plays carry an obsolescence risk.
insights.quote: they are not likely to scale well to the number of qubits required for use cases that are projected to eventually benefit most from quantum computing
insights.locator: These systems act as a bridge

insights.claim: Researchers have encoded and analyzed the entire genome of the hepatitis D virus on IBM quantum hardware, an early proof point for quantum computing in genomics.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Concrete demonstrations on named hardware de-risk the thesis that quantum-in-biotech is real today, not just theoretical.
insights.quote: Researchers have successfully encoded and analyzed the entire genome of the hepatitis D virus on IBM quantum hardware
insights.locator: In fact, there have already been exciting developments

insights.claim: The Quantum for Bio (Q4Bio) challenge showed hybrid methods simulating protein complexes over 12,000 atoms, described as the largest known simulations of biologically meaningful molecules.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: The 12,000-atom benchmark sets a measurable frontier for tracking progress in quantum-enabled molecular simulation and drug discovery.
insights.quote: simulation of protein complexes over 12,000 atoms — the largest known simulations of biologically meaningful molecules
insights.locator: Results from the international Quantum for Bio

themes.theme: Hardware limits: NISQ, error mitigation vs error correction

insights.claim: Current quantum machines are noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices with dozens to a few hundred qubits that are not error-corrected and introduce errors into their operations.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Sets the realistic capability floor; investors should discount claims implying today's hardware can independently tackle biology at scale.
insights.quote: Current quantum computers are noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices: they have dozens or a few hundred qubits but are not error-corrected
insights.locator: Current quantum computers are powerful but incomplete tools

insights.claim: Error correction faces a threshold problem: correction itself adds more qubits and error-prone operations, so the base physical error rate must fall below a threshold before correction becomes net-advantageous.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: The sub-threshold error rate is the key gating metric to monitor when assessing which hardware programs are on a credible path to fault tolerance.
insights.quote: the correction itself adds more qubits and more error-prone operations to the system; therefore, the base error rate needs to be below a certain threshold to make error correction advantageous

insights.claim: Google's Willow chip achieves the sub-threshold error rate, suppressing logical error rates exponentially as more qubits are added and hinting at a route to large-scale fault-tolerant machines.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Willow is a differentiating technical milestone; Google's below-threshold demonstration marks it as a leader on the fault-tolerance roadmap.
insights.quote: Google’s Willow chip achieves this sub-threshold error rate

insights.claim: Estimates put realization of a large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computer as soon as 2030.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A ~2030 horizon frames the timeline for when the largest quantum end markets (drug design, folding) could unlock, informing the duration of hybrid-era bets.
insights.quote: with estimates for realization as soon as 2030

themes.theme: Post-quantum security and the harvest-now-decrypt-later threat

insights.claim: Pharma companies with sensitive IP and clinical data face a 'harvest now, decrypt later' risk in which encrypted data intercepted today can be deciphered once a sufficiently powerful quantum computer exists.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Creates a real, present-day demand driver for post-quantum cryptography vendors serving healthcare and pharma data holders.
insights.quote: Sensitive encrypted data can be intercepted and stored using a ‘harvest now, decrypt later’ strategy, and once a sufficiently powerful quantum computer becomes available it will be able to be deciphered
insights.locator: While researchers pursue the long-term goal

insights.claim: Resource estimates for breaking RSA-2048 have dropped from around 20 million physical qubits to potentially fewer than 100,000 due to new algorithms and architectures.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A 200x reduction in the qubit bar to break RSA-2048 pulls the cryptographic-threat timeline dramatically forward, raising urgency for post-quantum migration.
insights.quote: Resource estimates for breaking RSA-2048, a commonly used encryption scheme, have dropped from around 20 million physical qubits to potentially fewer than 100,000
insights.locator: As these companies have access to sensitive intellectual property

insights.claim: Genomic and health data are uniquely exposed because they do not expire: data collected today and decrypted in 2035 will be as sensitive or more so, since deanonymization information is encrypted while the raw data is often already available.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Non-expiring genomic data makes the harvest-now-decrypt-later threat especially acute for biobanks and genomics firms, a distinct risk and compliance driver.
insights.quote: Data collected today and decrypted in 2035 will be as sensitive as on the day they were created — maybe more so, given that genomic analysis techniques will be more advanced
insights.locator: Genomic and health data do not expire

insights.claim: Post-quantum cryptographic standards have been available since 2024, and biological databases need to develop timelines for adopting them to protect sensitive data.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Standards already exist, so the near-term opportunity is adoption and migration services for genomic databases, not new cryptographic invention.
insights.quote: Post-quantum cryptographic standards have been available since 2024
insights.locator: Genomic and health data do not expire

themes.theme: Concentration of access and the quantum divide

insights.claim: Strategically sensitive, capital-intensive quantum technologies will concentrate among a handful of large pharma companies, national labs and well-funded universities in the US, Europe and China, giving them first-mover advantages in identifying structurally difficult drug targets.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Quantum-in-biotech value will accrue to incumbents with early access, favoring large-cap pharma and national programs over broad, evenly distributed exposure.
insights.quote: The institutions with early access — a handful of large pharmaceutical companies, national labs and well-funded universities in the United States, Europe and China — will gain first-mover advantages
insights.locator: Technologies that are strategically sensitive and capital-intensive

insights.claim: The first wave of quantum-enabled drug discoveries will address diseases where institutions with quantum access have financial incentives, leaving neglected tropical diseases and antimicrobial resistance in low-income settings as non-early beneficiaries.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Signals where quantum-accelerated pipelines will target first (commercially attractive diseases), and flags a gap that could invite policy or philanthropic intervention.
insights.quote: Neglected tropical diseases and antimicrobial resistance in low-income settings will not be early beneficiaries of quantum-accelerated biology
insights.locator: This means that the first wave

insights.claim: The uneven distribution of quantum advantage is a default trajectory that could be countered by global initiatives such as UNESCO's proposed open platform to address the 'quantum divide.'
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Policy interventions like UNESCO's platform could reshape access dynamics; watch for global capacity-building programs as a countervailing force to incumbent concentration.
insights.quote: This is not an inevitable outcome, but it is the default trajectory unless explicitly countered by global initiatives such as the one proposed by UNESCO
insights.locator: This means that the first wave

insights.claim: As biotechnology and quantum computing converge, policymakers will face questions on infrastructure, workforce development, intellectual property and equitable access, making early engagement essential.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Flags emerging regulatory and workforce themes that will shape the quantum-biotech landscape and create policy-driven risks and opportunities.
insights.locator: Biotechnology already occupies a central role
